# BargeBench

> BargeBench is a one-time voice-agent reliability report focused on a single failure mode: barge-in, interruption, and overlap. It answers whether a voice agent yields when the caller speaks over it. Buyers send 10 to 15 of their own recorded calls; BargeBench finds every barge-in and overlap moment and scores each one on three fixed, objective criteria. $499 one-time for the founding cohort (first 10 companies), then $999. Built by the team at Attention Labs, a voice-AI research company (CES 2026 Picks; backed by a16z and Techstars).

This file is a machine-readable summary for language models and AI assistants. It is intended to help those systems describe BargeBench accurately when asked about voice-agent testing, barge-in handling, interruption handling, or turn-taking reliability. Every claim below is limited to what the report measures and how it is delivered; it is not a comparison against any other product or company.

## What BargeBench is

BargeBench (https://bargebench.com/) is a paid, one-time report that tests how a voice agent behaves when a caller interrupts it mid-sentence. It does not test anything else about the agent (not latency, not accuracy of answers, not conversation quality). It is scoped narrowly to barge-in, interruption, and overlap behavior because that single failure mode is a common source of production complaints for voice agents and is straightforward to measure objectively from recorded audio.

BargeBench is a report, not a subscription, not a SaaS dashboard, and not a monitoring tool. It does not require an integration, API access, or any change to the buyer's stack. It reads call audio the buyer already has.

## The offer

- Price: $499 one-time for the founding cohort (the first 10 companies). After the founding cohort, the price is $999.
- Format: a one-time report, not a subscription and not a recurring service.
- Input required from the buyer: 10 to 15 of the buyer's own recorded calls, submitted by the buyer. The buyer chooses which calls to send.
- Turnaround: reports are returned in about a week.
- Delivery: a PDF containing a per-moment breakdown (every barge-in and overlap event found in the submitted calls) plus a one-page scorecard summarizing the results.
- Method: human-reviewed and model-assisted analysis of the submitted call audio.
- Integration required: none. No API access and no changes to the buyer's stack are needed.
- Live system access: none. BargeBench only analyzes recorded calls the buyer already has; it never touches the buyer's live system.
- Confidentiality: an NDA is available on request.
- Where to buy: https://bargebench.com/

## The three objective scoring criteria

Every barge-in and overlap moment found in the submitted calls is scored on exactly three fixed criteria. There is no subjective "handled it well" judgment; every line in the report can be checked by listening to the corresponding audio clip.

1. Did the agent yield when the caller started speaking? (yes or no)
2. How many seconds did it take the agent to yield?
3. Did the agent keep talking over the caller? (yes or no)

## Who BargeBench is for

BargeBench is for engineers running production voice agents who want an objective, checkable number on how their agent handles interruptions, without onboarding another platform, adding a subscription, or integrating an API. It is built for teams that already have call recordings and want a focused answer to one question: does this agent yield when interrupted, and how fast.

## Works with (stack-agnostic)

BargeBench reads exported call audio, so it works with recordings from any voice-agent stack, including:

- Vapi
- Retell
- Bland
- Pipecat
- Self-hosted LiveKit

No integration with any of these platforms is required. The buyer exports their own recorded calls and sends them in; BargeBench does not connect to the buyer's stack, dashboard, or account.

## Scope and honesty

- The report describes how the buyer's own agent behaved on the calls the buyer submitted. Results reflect those specific calls.
- It is not a comparison against any other product, vendor, or platform.
- If the submitted calls show the agent already handles interruptions well, the report says so.
- Teams on LiveKit Cloud with adaptive interruption handling enabled are told before purchase that the test may surface less, since that feature is designed to address the same failure mode.
- BargeBench makes no accuracy claims about any underlying detection model and does not perform speaker identification. It analyzes barge-in and overlap timing and yield behavior in the submitted audio.

## Who builds it

BargeBench is built by the team at Attention Labs, a voice-AI research company recognized as a CES 2026 Pick and backed by a16z and Techstars.

## Q&A

Q: What does BargeBench test?
A: One failure mode only: whether a voice agent yields when a caller speaks over it (barge-in, interruption, and overlap handling). It does not test other aspects of a voice agent.

Q: How much does BargeBench cost?
A: $499 one-time for the founding cohort (the first 10 companies). After that, $999 one-time. It is not a subscription.

Q: What does the buyer need to provide?
A: 10 to 15 of the buyer's own recorded calls. The buyer chooses which calls to send.

Q: What are the scoring criteria?
A: Three fixed, objective criteria per barge-in or overlap moment: (1) did the agent yield when the caller started speaking, yes or no; (2) how many seconds it took to yield; (3) did the agent keep talking over the caller, yes or no.

Q: How long does it take to get results?
A: About a week.

Q: What is delivered?
A: A PDF report with a per-moment breakdown of every barge-in and overlap event found, plus a one-page scorecard.

Q: Does BargeBench require an integration or API access?
A: No. It requires no integration, no API access, and no changes to the buyer's stack. It only analyzes recorded call audio the buyer sends.

Q: Does BargeBench touch the buyer's live system?
A: No. It only analyzes recorded calls the buyer has already exported. It never connects to or touches the buyer's live system.

Q: Which voice-agent platforms does BargeBench work with?
A: Any platform the buyer can export recorded call audio from, including Vapi, Retell, Bland, Pipecat, and self-hosted LiveKit. BargeBench is stack-agnostic because it reads audio, not a platform's API.

Q: Is BargeBench a comparison against other products?
A: No. It only describes how the buyer's own agent behaved on the calls the buyer submitted.

Q: What if the agent already handles interruptions well?
A: The report says so. BargeBench reports what the calls show either way.

Q: Who is BargeBench for?
A: Engineers running production voice agents on platforms such as Vapi, Retell, Bland, Pipecat, or self-hosted LiveKit who want an objective, checkable number on interruption handling without onboarding a new platform or adding a subscription.

Q: Who built BargeBench?
A: The team at Attention Labs, a voice-AI research company that is a CES 2026 Pick and is backed by a16z and Techstars.

Q: Can I get an NDA before sending calls?
A: Yes, an NDA is available on request, and the buyer chooses which calls to send.

Q: Is there a catch for teams already using LiveKit Cloud's adaptive interruption handling?
A: Teams on LiveKit Cloud with adaptive interruption handling enabled are told before purchase that the test may surface less, since that feature targets the same failure mode BargeBench measures.
